danger_signals.visual_form: Colored pill halves accumulating toward the narrow neck of the medicine bottle
danger_signals.meaning: Imminent game over; playfield saturation indicates loss condition

danger_signals.visual_form: Three distinct colored viruses (red, yellow, blue) positioned in the bottle
danger_signals.meaning: Active threat requiring elimination; each virus is an objective obstacle

danger_signals.visual_form: Single-colored pill halves unsupported or falling without matching counterparts
danger_signals.meaning: Failed alignment; wasted space and reduced playfield efficiency

safety_signals.visual_form: Empty spaces in the bottle where viruses have been eliminated
safety_signals.meaning: Progress toward level completion; reduced threat density

safety_signals.visual_form: Multiple 4-in-a-row matches cascading as capsules fall and realign
safety_signals.meaning: Positive momentum; bonus points and efficient virus elimination

safety_signals.visual_form: Victory animations showing virus trio in pastoral settings with flying objects
safety_signals.meaning: Milestone achieved; transition to next level; world-state advancement

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Mario character identity; medical authority through doctor role
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Medicine bottle as the arena; capsules as tools of intervention

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Distinct virus sprites occupying specific positions
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Color differentiation serves as faction/type identifier
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Viruses remain stationary in bottle until matched and destroyed

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Fewer viruses distributed across the bottle; more empty space; narrow neck clearly visible and unobstructed
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Capsules fall at slower speed (selectable); player has strategic flexibility

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Increasing virus density; partial bottle fill; emerging capsule accumulation
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Falling speed increases with difficulty; chain reactions become more critical

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: All viruses removed; bottle cleared; cutscene trigger shows pastoral scene with virus characters safe distance away
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Game transitions to next level; score calculation; difficulty increase on subsequent levels

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Bottle neck completely blocked by unmatched capsules; playfield saturation with mixed colors
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Game halts; player loses round; multiplayer opponent gains advantage or wins

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Dual playfields displayed side-by-side; penalty capsules visually differ from standard falling capsules when sent to opponent
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Successful chain reactions trigger opponent field changes; competitive interaction manifests through visual pollution

environmental_storytelling.example: Medicine bottle container, colored capsules resembling pharmaceutical pills, virus adversaries positioned as medical pathogens—all communicate the implicit narrative that gameplay is a medical intervention

After each 5th level (up to level 20), cutscenes depict the three-colored virus characters in pastoral settings—suggesting peaceful existence before and after medical intervention, establishing loose narrative context without dialogue

Red, yellow, and blue virus variants establish three distinct threat categories; matching capsule colors to viruses creates implicit understanding of targeting and resolution mechanics through visual language alone
The medicine bottle's narrow neck serves both mechanical function (lose condition) and environmental storytelling—visually communicates containment failure as the game-over state

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Stationary; positioned in fixed locations within bottle; visually inert until matched
Disappear with visual effect (starburst animation); removed from playfield
Descend vertically; respond to left/right movement input; rotate 90 degrees on command; fall speed varies by difficulty setting
Vanish; trigger chain reaction if adjacent matches exist; surviving halves fall to re-establish support
Physics-based drop until hitting supported object or bottle floor; create cascading match potential
Visual tension increases; player loses when neck becomes blocked; game-over condition triggers immediately

entity_behavioral_tells.state: Chain reaction triggered on player side
entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Penalty capsules automatically sent to opponent; visible in opponent's playfield as unwanted additions; manifests as a tangible competitive consequence

notes: Dr. Mario uses minimalist visual communication typical of 1990 NES puzzle games. The game world has no faction-based conflict in a narrative sense—instead, viruses and capsules represent medical problem/solution duality. Visual manifestation of world-state relies entirely on playfield saturation, color alignment, and cutscene interludes. The game lacks explicit danger or safety UI elements; all information is conveyed through core gameplay visuals. Multiplayer mode introduces competitive state-sharing through opponent field pollution, creating implicit 'attack' visuals without explicit conflict animation. The three-color virus system and matching mechanic create non-narrative 'faction' distinction through color-coding alone.
